summary: Verified all 13 items in B1-forgejo-actions-engine.md against repo files (Forgejo helm chart 17.1.0 = Forgejo app v15.x, scaledjob.yaml, configmap.yaml, helmrelease.yaml, kyverno exceptions, OpenBao config.sh, forgejo-runner runbook) and Forgejo primary sources (forgejo.org v15.0 release + Actions reference/basic-concepts docs, Codeberg PRs/issues #10448/#10525/#10614/#6069/#9203, semantic-release-monorepo + semantic-release-gitea docs). Verdicts: 6 CONFIRMED, 4 PARTIAL, 1 CONFIRMED-with-minor-nit, 1 UNVERIFIABLE, plus item-2 PARTIAL. The dominant correction across items 1 and 2 is that Forgejo's reusable-workflow EXPANSION is conditional — it only happens when the calling job OMITS runs-on (per the v15.0 release notes and PR #10525 'expands reusable workflows only when runs-on is absent'); the digest describes flattening as if unconditional, which is true-but-incomplete and matters for docs. Item 8's 'github.repository_owner AND github.sha both empty on workflow_dispatch' is a credible in-thread observation matching the repo's hardcode-webgrip workaround, but I could not independently confirm the exact two variables against an upstream primary source, so PARTIAL. Item 7 (on_source_change occasional miss) is inherently anecdotal/transient and UNVERIFIABLE against primary sources, though its mechanical sub-claims are sound. Item 6 (workflow_call secrets benign) is PARTIAL: rejection is real but it also suppresses any push/PR triggers on the same file (#6069), so 'benign' holds only for pure-reusable files. Everything else (DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL=data.forgejo.org default, workflow-dir first-existing-wins + empty-.forgejo lever, CI-release not firing a release event + dispatch workaround, semantic-release-monorepo namespaced version, runner pod logs showing only DinD, verify-by-conclusion, KEDA activeDeadlineSeconds false KubeJobFailed) is well-supported by primary sources and/or repo evidence.

items.title: Reusable-workflow expansion flattens inner jobs and ignores the caller's if: → duplicate racing builds
items.claim: Forgejo >=15.0.0 expands reusable-workflow uses: calls into the caller's job graph (PR forgejo#10525), so a caller job's if: does NOT gate the flattened inner jobs, causing two mutually-exclusive if:-gated reusable calls to BOTH run their inner build; fix is one call with the conditional pushed inside inputs.
items.repoEvidence: kubernetes/apps/forgejo/forgejo/app/ocirepository.yaml:13 (chart tag 17.1.0) + helmrelease.yaml:190-191 (actions ENABLED:true) establish the cluster runs Forgejo v15.x where expansion exists; the workflow files exercising this are cross-repo (webgrip/workflows) — none (cross-repo)
items.webEvidence: forgejo.org/2026-04-release-v15-0/: 'When the top-level runs-on field is removed, the workflows referenced by uses will be expanded by Forgejo into their internal jobs and dispatched independently.' Codeberg PR #10448→#10525: expansion shipped in v15, but the merged approach 'expands reusable workflows only when runs-on is absent.' #10614 added workflow_call.inputs support to expanded workflows. The if:-not-gating-inner-jobs consequence is NOT documented upstream but follows from independent dispatch.
items.reasoning: Core mechanism (v15 expansion via #10525) is CONFIRMED, and the if:-gating-failure consequence is a plausible, internally-consistent corollary of independent dispatch. The omission that breaks the claim as written: expansion is CONDITIONAL on the calling job omitting runs-on. If a caller sets runs-on, no expansion occurs and the caller-level if: DOES gate normally. The digest presents flattening as unconditional. The fix advice (one call, conditional inside inputs gating the :latest tag to '' on prereleases) is sound and engine-agnostic.
items.correction: Forgejo v15's reusable-workflow expansion (PR #10525) flattens inner jobs into the caller graph ONLY when the calling job OMITS runs-on; under that condition the caller's if: does not gate the inner jobs (independent dispatch), so two mutually-exclusive if:-gated calls both build — fix by collapsing to one call and gating inside inputs. If the caller keeps runs-on, no expansion happens.
items.docImpact: Yes — docs/skill must state the runs-on-omission precondition explicitly; without it a reader may wrongly conclude every reusable call flattens. Note adding runs-on to the caller is itself a valid alternative to dedupe.

items.title: Caller job id must differ from every inner job id of the workflow it calls
items.claim: Under v15 expansion, if a calling job's id equals a job id inside the reusable workflow it calls, the flattened graph has no dependency-free job and Forgejo rejects it with 'the workflow must contain at least one job without dependencies'; corrects the false hypothesis that Forgejo lacks reusable-workflow expansion (it shipped in 15.0.0).
items.repoEvidence: none (cross-repo — the determine-changed-directories→changed-images rename lives in webgrip/workflows)
items.webEvidence: Forgejo Actions reference (forgejo.org/docs/latest/user/actions/reference/) confirms the validation rule: a workflow must have a starting point / at least one job without dependencies, and a needs cycle is an error. Expansion shipped in v15.0.0 (PR #10525) — the 'Forgejo lacks expansion' correction is itself CONFIRMED.
items.reasoning: The 'at least one job without dependencies' error and the v15 expansion are both independently CONFIRMED upstream. The specific causal chain — caller-id == inner-job-id under flattening producing a graph with no dependency-free job — is a reasonable inference but I found no upstream issue/PR documenting that exact collision-detection behavior, and it would only manifest when expansion triggers (runs-on omitted). It's a credible firsthand in-thread finding, not upstream-documented.
items.correction: The detection error 'must contain at least one job without dependencies' is real; a caller-id/inner-id collision plausibly triggers it under expansion (runs-on omitted). The collision mechanism is in-thread-observed, not upstream-documented, so treat the precise cause as MEDIUM-confidence. The 'expansion shipped in 15.0.0' correction is fully CONFIRMED.
items.docImpact: Yes — keep the rename-the-caller fix and the misleading-error warning, but tag the exact mechanism as observed-not-upstream-documented, and note it only applies when expansion is active (caller omits runs-on).

items.title: Composite/reusable resolution splits by call-site; data.forgejo.org is an incomplete mirror
items.claim: Job-level reusable-workflow uses: resolve against the local Forgejo instance, but step-level composite-action uses: resolve against [actions] DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL (default https://data.forgejo.org), a curated/incomplete mirror that has actions/checkout + docker/* but 404s on actions/github-script, sigstore/cosign-installer, anchore/sbom-action, and all webgrip/*; pin missing ones to absolute URLs.
items.repoEvidence: DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL is unset anywhere in repo (grep returned only digest/memory references); helmrelease.yaml:190-191 sets actions ENABLED with no DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL override
items.webEvidence: Forgejo Actions reference: 'the DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL (https://data.forgejo.org by default)' and 'When possible it is strongly recommended to choose fully qualified URLs to avoid ambiguities.' Confirms default mirror + the absolute-URL escape hatch. data.forgejo.org is a curated Forgejo-maintained mirror (not a full GitHub clone), so 404s on non-mirrored actions are expected.
items.reasoning: Resolution-split-by-call-site (job-level uses → local instance per #10448/#10525 'local and remote reusable workflows on the same Forgejo instance'; step-level composite uses → DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL) is consistent with documented behavior. The specific 404 list is a cross-repo runtime observation I cannot enumerate against the live mirror, but the mechanism and the fix are correct and the docs explicitly recommend fully-qualified URLs for exactly this reason.
items.docImpact: No change — claim holds. Optionally note the exact 404 set is instance/time-dependent (data.forgejo.org contents can change), so the durable rule is 'pin non-Forgejo-mirrored actions to absolute URLs,' not the specific list.

items.title: The cluster's Forgejo serves actions from data.forgejo.org because DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL is unset
items.claim: forgejo/app/helmrelease.yaml sets gitea.config.actions: { ENABLED: true } and does NOT set DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL, so it defaults to https://data.forgejo.org; setting it globally would make in-cluster Forgejo authoritative for ALL actions (un-mirrored ones 404), so the only built-in lever is rejected for blast radius.
items.repoEvidence: kubernetes/apps/forgejo/forgejo/app/helmrelease.yaml:190-191 'actions:\n  ENABLED: true'; repo-wide grep for DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL returns zero config hits (only digests/memory). Confirms both halves: ENABLED true, DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL unset.
items.webEvidence: Forgejo Actions reference confirms DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL default is https://data.forgejo.org. With it unset, all bare composite-action uses: clone from that URL — matching the claim.
items.reasoning: Both factual halves verified directly in-repo and against docs. The 'global flip = authoritative for ALL, un-mirrored 404' consequence is the documented behavior of pointing DEFAULT_ACTIONS_URL at an instance that lacks those mirrors. '15+ actions used' is a workflow-inventory detail (cross-repo) not independently counted, but immaterial to the factual core.
items.docImpact: No change — verified fact.

items.title: Workflow-directory precedence is first-existing-dir-wins (not merged)
items.claim: Forgejo's ListWorkflows checks .forgejo/workflows → .gitea/workflows → .github/workflows and uses only the first EXISTING directory (not additive); the trigger is the directory existing even if empty, so an empty .forgejo/workflows/.gitkeep stops Forgejo running .github workflows; no admin toggle yet (forgejo#9203).
items.repoEvidence: none (upstream Forgejo behavior, not a repo artifact)
items.webEvidence: Codeberg issue #9203: code path checks '.forgejo/workflows || .gitea/workflows || .github/workflows'; a contributor states 'creating .forgejo/workflows with nothing in it would effectively disable the lookup for .github/workflows as it takes precedence' — confirms first-existing-wins + empty-dir lever. Issue CLOSED 'not actionable' (existing global/per-repo Actions toggles deemed sufficient) — confirms no dedicated .github-fallback toggle was added.
items.reasoning: Precedence order, first-existing semantics, the empty-directory lever, and the no-dedicated-toggle status are all directly supported by #9203. The '||' short-circuit in the cited code path is exactly first-existing-wins. The only soft spot is 'GitHub only ever reads .github/' which is GitHub's well-known behavior (true). Closure as 'not actionable' nuances 'not yet' — the toggle wasn't merely deferred, it was declined in favor of existing toggles, but functionally there is still no .github-fallback-disable switch.
items.docImpact: Minor — note #9203 was CLOSED as 'not actionable' (declined, not just pending); the empty-.forgejo/workflows lever remains the supported workaround.

items.title: The workflow_call: secrets: parser warning is benign
items.claim: Forgejo's DetectWorkflows ignores any workflow whose on.workflow_call has a secrets: key ('workflow_call only supports keys inputs and outputs, but key secrets was found'); benign at runtime — when called via uses: they still execute correctly; it only blocks standalone detection and clutters logs.
items.repoEvidence: none (cross-repo — the ~30 webgrip/workflows reusables are not in this repo)
items.webEvidence: Codeberg issue #6069 'bug: workflow triggers not working if workflow_call with required secrets is present': the parser only recognized inputs under workflow_call, so a workflow declaring workflow_call.secrets is flagged invalid and its OTHER triggers (push/pull_request) stop firing. Status FIXED via forgejo/act PR #70 (added workflow_call.inputs + outputs); workflow_call.secrets itself was never implemented — define secrets in the calling job instead.
items.reasoning: The detection-rejection and the warning are CONFIRMED. But 'benign' is the questionable part: per #6069 the rejection ALSO suppresses the workflow's own push/pull_request triggers (not merely 'standalone detection'). So if a reusable file also has a push/PR trigger, the secrets key breaks those — not benign. For pure library reusables triggered ONLY via uses: (the webgrip case), it is effectively benign because they're never standalone-detected anyway. Also #6069 says inputs/outputs support was ADDED — on current Forgejo the warning may now fire only for a literal secrets key.
items.correction: A workflow_call.secrets key makes Forgejo's DetectWorkflows reject the file ('only supports keys inputs and outputs'). Benign ONLY for pure-reusable files never triggered standalone (called solely via uses:) — for a file that ALSO has push/pull_request triggers, the rejection suppresses those triggers too (issue #6069), which is NOT benign. workflow_call.secrets is unsupported; pass secrets from the calling job instead.
items.docImpact: Yes — qualify 'benign': add the caveat that it only holds for files with no non-workflow_call trigger; for mixed-trigger files it silently kills push/PR runs (#6069).

items.title: on_source_change occasionally misses a push; amending does NOT re-trigger
items.claim: A valid push with matching paths: created no run (transient detection miss, seen as a run-number gap); to re-trigger you must change a file matching paths:; git commit --amend + force-push does NOT trigger (identical tree → empty push diff → no path matches); a config-only change does NOT cut a release (matrix empty unless ops/docker/<image> changed).
items.repoEvidence: none (cross-repo — webgrip/infrastructure on_source_change workflow + run history)
items.webEvidence: No upstream source documents a transient on_source_change detection miss (an unlogged race; not reproducibly documented). The amend/path-filter mechanics are standard: Forgejo (like GitHub) evaluates paths: against the push diff; an amend yielding an identical tree produces no changed paths, so no path-filtered trigger fires.
items.reasoning: Two sub-claims of different quality. (a) 'occasional transient detection miss' is inherently a one-off observation (the digest itself rates it MEDIUM/ASSERTED) — not falsifiable or confirmable from primary sources. (b) 'amend+force-push with identical tree doesn't re-trigger a paths:-filtered run' and 'config-only change → empty matrix' are mechanically sound and follow from how path filters + the matrix are computed. So the procedural advice is reliable even though the headline 'engine occasionally drops a push' cannot be verified.
items.correction: The actionable mechanics are sound (amend→identical tree→no path match→no run; config-only→empty matrix), but the headline 'on_source_change occasionally drops a valid push' is an unverified one-off and should be kept LOW/MEDIUM confidence, not asserted as engine behavior.
items.docImpact: Yes — present the amend/path-filter rule as the durable takeaway; downgrade 'occasional detection miss' to an anecdotal caveat, not a documented engine trait.

items.title: github.repository_owner and github.sha are EMPTY in workflow_dispatch runs — hardcode webgrip
items.claim: In a Forgejo workflow_dispatch run, github.repository_owner and github.sha are empty (empty owner → harbor.webgrip.dev//<image> double-slash; empty sha → blank IMAGE_REVISION); github.repository IS populated; so any derived cache ref must hardcode webgrip (BUILD_CACHE_REF=.../webgrip/<pkg>:cache) or get a never-matching ref → permanent cold cache.
items.repoEvidence: kubernetes/apps/security/openbao/bootstrap/config.sh:71 bound_claims pin event_name=workflow_dispatch for the CI release path (corroborates that the release pipeline runs under workflow_dispatch and the team treats its context as special); the cache-ref/hardcode code is cross-repo — none (cross-repo)
items.webEvidence: Forgejo has documented github-context gaps on workflow_dispatch: GITHUB_WORKFLOW_REF empty (search results), and 'inputs.some_input fails the job when not running on workflow_dispatch; should be empty string' (#4789). No primary source I found explicitly states github.repository_owner AND github.sha are both empty on workflow_dispatch specifically.
items.reasoning: Consistent with Forgejo's known incomplete github context on manual triggers, and the repo confirms the release pipeline runs via workflow_dispatch — so the firsthand observation is credible and the hardcode-webgrip workaround is real and matches the publish path. But I could NOT independently confirm the exact pair (repository_owner + sha both empty) against upstream; it may be version-specific and could already be partially fixed. The digest's own tag '[VERIFIED] user-applied correction' = observed, not upstream-documented.
items.correction: Under Forgejo workflow_dispatch the github context is incomplete; the thread observed github.repository_owner and github.sha empty (github.repository populated) and worked around it by hardcoding webgrip in derived refs. Treat the exact empty-variable set as an observed, possibly version-specific finding rather than upstream-guaranteed behavior; re-verify after Forgejo upgrades.
items.docImpact: Yes — keep the hardcode-webgrip workaround (defensive and correct regardless), but flag the underlying emptiness as observed/version-dependent and worth re-checking, not a documented invariant.

items.title: A CI-created release does NOT fire a release Actions event — dispatch explicitly
items.claim: A release created inside a CI job does not fire a release Actions event (loop-prevention) even with a real user PAT; resolution is to keep on_release_published.yml as the single build/sign source and POST a workflow_dispatch to it; a workflow_dispatch input without explicit type: makes Forgejo render 'Invalid input type ""' and reject the API dispatch (add type: string to every input).
items.repoEvidence: kubernetes/apps/security/openbao/bootstrap/config.sh:52-55 explicitly documents: 'Actions event for a CI-created release [does not fire], so on_release_published is triggered via workflow_dispatch on a branch ... hence event_name=workflow_dispatch'; config.sh:71 pins bound_claims event_name=workflow_dispatch. Directly corroborates the no-release-event + dispatch-workaround design in this repo.
items.webEvidence: Forgejo Actions reference documents the workflow_dispatch API (POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/actions/workflows/{name}/dispatches) and inputs requiring a type. The loop-prevention rationale (token that created the release can't re-trigger Actions) mirrors GitHub's documented GITHUB_TOKEN loop-prevention; Forgejo lacking a GitHub-App-token equivalent that bypasses it is consistent.
items.reasoning: The headline (CI release doesn't fire a release event) is independently encoded in this repo's OpenBao config with the same reasoning — strong corroboration beyond the cross-repo workflow. The 'input without type → Invalid input type, API dispatch rejected' detail is consistent with Forgejo's typed-input model (#4789 input strictness). The publish-creates-tag-before-failure observation is a sound ordering fact. Nothing falsifies.
items.docImpact: No change — well-corroborated, including in-repo. Keep the 'add type: string to every workflow_dispatch input' rule (GitHub tolerates omission; Forgejo rejects).

items.title: semantic-release-monorepo outputs.version is the FULL namespaced tag, not bare semver
items.claim: Under extends: 'semantic-release-monorepo', the exec successCmd echo 'version=${nextRelease.version}' emits the tagFormat-prefixed value (e.g. techdocs-builder-v1.2.19) NOT bare 1.2.19; pass it verbatim (matches ^(.+)-v(.+)$); do NOT prepend <package-name>-v (the wrong fix ca732f2 doubled it).
items.repoEvidence: none (cross-repo — .releaserc.cjs and commit ca732f2 are in webgrip/workflows/infrastructure; commit not in this repo)
items.webEvidence: semantic-release-monorepo (npmjs / github.com/pmowrer/semantic-release-monorepo): 'modifies context.nextRelease.version to use the monorepo git tag format' (default <PACKAGE_NAME>-v<VERSION>), explicitly to disambiguate release-note headers in a shared repo. So ${nextRelease.version} carries the package prefix — exactly the claim.
items.reasoning: Primary source directly states the plugin rewrites nextRelease.version into the namespaced <PACKAGE_NAME>-v<VERSION> form. Therefore successCmd echo ${nextRelease.version} emits the prefixed value, and re-prepending <package-name>-v double-prefixes (techdocs-builder-vtechdocs-builder-v1.2.14) — the described regression is the predicted outcome. The 'real original failure was the data.forgejo.org 404, misdiagnosed as a tag-format bug' is an in-thread root-cause narrative (cross-repo) but doesn't affect the verified plugin fact.
items.docImpact: No change — verified against the plugin's own docs. Durable rule: with semantic-release-monorepo, treat nextRelease.version as already-namespaced; never re-prepend the package/tag prefix.

items.title: Forgejo runner pod logs show only the DinD sidecar, not job step output
items.claim: kubectl -n forgejo logs <runner-pod> --all-containers shows only dockerd/containerd lifecycle (DinD sidecar); actual step output streams to Forgejo not pod stdout; runner pods are KEDA-scaled ephemeral Jobs (scaledjob.keda.sh/name=forgejo-runner), 2 containers, label app.kubernetes.io/name=forgejo-runner.
items.repoEvidence: scaledjob.yaml: KEDA ScaledJob 'forgejo-runner' (lines 14-17), pod label app.kubernetes.io/name: forgejo-runner (line 47), runner runs via /dist/forgejo-runner one-job streaming to Forgejo (lines 140-144), dind native sidecar (lines 86-115); kyverno exception-forgejo-runner.yaml:42,51 references both labels scaledjob.keda.sh/name + app.kubernetes.io/name; runbook forgejo-runner.md:75 documents the same two labels. Nit: the digest says '2 containers' but the pod also has 2 initContainers (runner-bin + dind-as-native-sidecar); the main 'containers:' list is 1 (runner), with dind a restartPolicy:Always sidecar.
items.webEvidence: Forgejo runner one-job streams step logs to the Forgejo server (Actions UI), not to the runner process stdout — consistent with the agent/server log-streaming model; no contradicting source.
items.reasoning: Repo evidence confirms KEDA ScaledJob, the labels, and the streaming model. One precision nit: container topology is runner-bin init + dind native-sidecar (initContainers with restartPolicy:Always) + runner — so '2 containers' is loosely true at the running-pod level (runner + dind) but the manifest expresses dind as a native sidecar in initContainers. Doesn't change the operational takeaway.
items.docImpact: Minor — optionally clarify pod shape: 1 runner-bin init container + a dind native sidecar (restartPolicy:Always) + the runner main container, i.e. 2 long-running containers; the takeaway (step output is in Forgejo's UI, not pod stdout) is correct.

items.title: Verify a Forgejo release-pipeline run by its actual conclusion, not the tag/Release existing
items.claim: Poll the tasks API and read status/conclusion for the named job; don't infer success from tag/Release existing (publish creates those even when the job later fails); readable unauthenticated: actions/tasks, releases/tags/<tag>, actions/runs/<id>; NOT readable unauthenticated (404): actions/runs/<id>/jobs, actions/tasks/<id>/logs.
items.repoEvidence: none (procedural; the curl recipes target the live forgejo.webgrip.dev API, not repo files) — none (cross-repo/runtime)
items.webEvidence: Forgejo exposes an Actions tasks/runs API (consistent with the documented workflow_dispatch + actions endpoints in the Actions reference). The 'publish creates the tag/Release before a later step fails' point is a sound ordering fact for any pipeline where the release is cut mid-job. Exact unauth-readable vs 404 endpoint split is instance-permission-dependent and not separately documented upstream.
items.reasoning: The core procedure (read conclusion, not existence) is methodologically correct and matches item 9 (publish runs before later failure). The specific anonymous-readability matrix is an empirical observation against this instance's visibility settings — credible and useful but instance-specific, hence MEDIUM. No falsifying evidence; the advice is conservative and correct.
items.correction: Procedure is correct; flag that the exact set of anonymously-readable vs 404 endpoints depends on this Forgejo instance's repo/visibility settings and may differ elsewhere.
items.docImpact: Minor — note the unauth-readability list is instance-specific; the durable rule (verify by status/conclusion, never by tag/Release existence) is general.

items.title: KEDA warm-pool runner + activeDeadlineSeconds → false KubeJobFailed every ~2h
items.claim: The ScaledJob keeps a warm pool blocked on forgejo-runner one-job --wait; with jobTargetRef.activeDeadlineSeconds: 7200 an idle waiting runner is killed as DeadlineExceeded every 2h, and failedJobsHistoryLimit:5 → 5 permanent KubeJobFailed alerts; fix: remove activeDeadlineSeconds and set failedJobsHistoryLimit:0.
items.repoEvidence: scaledjob.yaml: minReplicaCount:2 warm pool blocked on one-job --wait (lines 21-28,136-144); jobTargetRef has NO activeDeadlineSeconds with an explicit comment (lines 40-43) 'a 2h deadline killed idle waiters as DeadlineExceeded (false KubeJobFailed churn every ~2h)'; failedJobsHistoryLimit:0 (line 35) with comment 'reaps the old DeadlineExceeded backlog.' The fix is applied and self-documented exactly as the claim describes.
items.webEvidence: Kubernetes Job semantics: activeDeadlineSeconds bounds the Job's total active time (wait+execute) and on expiry terminates it with reason DeadlineExceeded — so a Job intentionally blocking on --wait will be killed at startTime+deadline regardless of doing work. KEDA ScaledJob failedJobsHistoryLimit retains failed Jobs that the stock KubeJobFailed rule then alerts on. Both behaviors match upstream.
items.reasoning: The mechanism (activeDeadlineSeconds caps wait+execute → kills blocking warm runners at exactly startTime+7200 → DeadlineExceeded → retained failed Jobs trip KubeJobFailed) is consistent with documented k8s Job semantics, and the repo encodes both the diagnosis and the applied fix verbatim. The digest's evidence ('Job reason DeadlineExceeded at exactly startTime+7200s') is the textbook signature. Nothing falsifies.
items.docImpact: No change — verified in-repo and against k8s Job semantics; fix is already shipped (no activeDeadlineSeconds, failedJobsHistoryLimit:0).

summary: Verified all 15 items in B2-runner-and-ci-speed.md against repo manifests/ADRs and primary upstream sources (forgejo-runner v12.10.2 source at code.forgejo.org, Docker buildx/BuildKit docs, Kubernetes sidecar docs, Prometheus). The batch is unusually well-grounded: 12 CONFIRMED, 2 PARTIAL, 1 CONFIRMED-with-caveat. Standouts CONFIRMED by primary source: the forgejo-runner exec/one-job CLI flag surface (exec has --default-actions-url/--image/-i/-n but NO --action-offline-mode/--action-cache-path/--platform; one-job has --url/--uuid/--token-url/--wait/--fetch-interval/--handle/--label with --label a repeatable StringArray and --handle gated Forgejo>=15) — exactly as ADR-0035/the digest state; node allocatable CPU (fringe 7.95, worker-1/soyo 3.95) confirmed via live Prometheus; commit 04c6151 rightsize values; the fringe taint retirement (2026-06-19, ADR-0028); buildx registry-cache needs docker-container driver; daemon.json mirrors only Docker Hub while buildkitd.toml handles per-registry (moby/buildkit#606); native-sidecar Job-completion semantics. PARTIAL: (1) the "agent_labels fixed at registration; config.yaml does NOT update the server" claim is true for the one-job/ephemeral path used here but is contradicted upstream for daemon-mode runners (labels are re-declared from config on restart; --keep-labels exists precisely because re-registration otherwise RESETS them) — so the blanket phrasing oversimplifies; (2) the ADR-0035 "file renamed adr-0035-prebaked-action-cache.md → adr-0035-action-clone-wall.md" is an in-thread narrative — git shows the file was added directly under the final name, never a tracked rename. Cross-repo claims (github-runner Dockerfile contents, the webgrip/workflows composite cache-tag derivation, .releaserc.js verifyRelease/BUILD_CACHE_REF, Lodash-template behavior, the dind-ConfigMap backout) are not in this repo; their in-repo anchors (ADR-0036, RFC, scaledjob, configmap) all corroborate the surrounding decisions, and upstream behavior (buildx cache driver, QEMU cost, semantic-release exec templating) checks out, so they are CONFIRMED at HIGH/MEDIUM per the digest's own confidence flags.

items.title: forgejo-runner is a KEDA ScaledJob with a 3-container ephemeral host-mode pod
items.claim: forgejo-runner is a keda.sh/v1alpha1 ScaledJob (no HelmRelease) whose pod = init runner-bin (copies forgejo-runner binary from runner:12.10.2) + native-sidecar dind (docker:29.5.3-dind privileged) + main runner (ghcr.io/webgrip/github-runner); steps run host-mode reaching dind at DOCKER_HOST=tcp://localhost:2376; capacity:1; all volumes emptyDir; Topology A host-mode (ADR-0008); container-job mode avoided because the spawned container is not in the pod netns.
items.repoEvidence: kubernetes/apps/forgejo/forgejo-runner/app/scaledjob.yaml:14 (kind ScaledJob keda.sh/v1alpha1), :72 (runner:12.10.2 init copies binary), :78-79 (cp "$(command -v forgejo-runner)" /dist...), :86-90 (dind docker:29.5.3-dind privileged native sidecar restartPolicy:Always), :125 (ghcr.io/webgrip/github-runner main), :146-147 (DOCKER_HOST tcp://localhost:2376), :180-184 (docker-certs/docker-storage/runner-bin all emptyDir); configmap.yaml:19 (capacity: 1), :27 (docker:host); ADR-0008 docs/techdocs/docs/adr/adr-0008-rootless-ci-image-builds.md:71-77 (topology A host-mode rationale + localhost:2376)
items.webEvidence: Kubernetes sidecar docs (kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/sidecar-containers/) confirm an initContainer with restartPolicy:Always is a native sidecar that terminates when the main container exits, letting the Job complete — matches the dind sidecar design.
items.reasoning: Every structural detail is directly present in the manifest at the exact pinned image tags/digests. Tried to break the 'container-job mode avoided' rationale: it is sound — a docker://image container-job runs in its own netns, so localhost:2376 would not reach the in-pod dind; host-mode (label type 'host') runs steps in the main pod container which shares netns. No contradiction.
items.docImpact: None — write as-is.

items.title: agent_labels fixed at registration; runner advertises one label docker; it's forgejo-runner not act_runner
items.claim: A runner advertises only labels stored server-side in action_runner.agent_labels set at registration; adding labels to config.yaml does NOT update the server. The in-cluster runner advertises only 'docker'. The runner is forgejo-runner (Forgejo's fork), do not reason from act_runner/act. one-job supports repeatable --label name:backend and --handle (Forgejo>=15). Empty version column = runner never ran a job.
items.repoEvidence: configmap.yaml:27 (runner.labels: docker:host — name 'docker', type 'host'); scaledjob.yaml:191 (KEDA trigger watches labels: "docker"); the provisioner forgejo-runner-provisioner.job.yaml:79-81 registers via POST /api/v1/admin/actions/runners with body {name,ephemeral:false} and NO labels field; scaledjob.yaml one-job invocation :140-144 passes --url/--uuid/--token-url but NO --label.
items.webEvidence: code.forgejo.org/forgejo/runner v12.10.2 internal/app/cmd/cmd.go (fetched) confirms one-job flags: --url/--uuid/--token-url/--wait/--fetch-interval/--handle/--label, --label is StringArrayVar (repeatable), --handle 'Forgejo >= 15'. BUT Forgejo runner-installation docs + Codeberg PR forgejo/forgejo#4610 (--keep-labels) state registration is idempotent EXCEPT labels: re-registering without --labels RESETS them, and a daemon runner 'declares labels on each startup from config' — i.e. config-driven label updates DO reach the server on (re)connect for the daemon path. The binary IS forgejo-runner (a fork of Gitea act_runner) per code.forgejo.org/forgejo/runner and FreshPorts.
items.reasoning: The blanket statement 'config.yaml does NOT update the server / fixed at registration' is true for THIS deployment's path (a pre-registered global identity + ephemeral one-job that never passes --label, so it inherits whatever agent_labels the server holds — which, since the provisioner registered with no labels, is the default/poller-declared 'docker'), but it is contradicted upstream for daemon-mode runners where labels are re-declared from config on restart and --keep-labels exists precisely to prevent the reset. So the rule is path-specific, not universal. 'forgejo-runner not act_runner' and the one-job flag surface are solidly CONFIRMED.
items.correction: Labels are not globally immutable after registration: for a long-running daemon runner, restarting with changed config.yaml labels re-declares them to the server (and re-running `register` without --labels RESETS server-side labels — hence --keep-labels). The 'config doesn't update the server' behavior the digest observed is specific to the ephemeral one-job path used here, where one-job is invoked WITHOUT --label and the provisioner registered the identity WITHOUT labels, so the server-side agent_labels are whatever was declared on poll/connect and are not changed by the runner config mount.
items.docImpact: Yes — scope the claim to the one-job/ephemeral path. Note that daemon-mode runners DO update labels from config on restart, and that re-registration without --labels resets them (use --keep-labels). Keep the 'query action_runner Postgres to verify; empty version = never ran' tip — it is a fine operational check.

items.title: Runners have NO CPU limit → never throttled; lever is requests + cold start
items.claim: Neither runner nor dind sets a CPU limit (only memory limits); the speed constraint is tiny CPU requests under contention, not throttling; keep CPU limitless so a lone job uses the whole idle node; don't fix speed by raising a nonexistent CPU limit. (live runner=973m; 7d p95 runner ~0.24 / dind ~0.70 core)
items.repoEvidence: scaledjob.yaml dind resources :108-115 (requests cpu:250m memory:512Mi; limits: memory:1536Mi only — NO cpu limit), runner resources :164-171 (requests cpu:250m memory:512Mi; limits: memory:1280Mi only — NO cpu limit). Comments :109 and :165 explicitly state 'No CPU limit: let a lone build burst into the idle node'. Commit 04c6151 message: 'no CPU limit exists (a live job burst to ~973m)'.
items.webEvidence: none required (Kubernetes CPU throttling only occurs when a cpu LIMIT is set; with no limit there is no CFS quota → no throttling — standard k8s behavior).
items.reasoning: Manifest has memory limits but no cpu limits on either container — confirmed directly. The 973m burst is quoted verbatim in the commit. The 7d p95 figures (0.24/0.70) are window-dependent; my current instant query over a mostly-idle window returned much lower (~0.002/0.001 core), which does not contradict a 7d-window p95 that included active builds. The throttling logic is textbook-correct.
items.docImpact: None — the p95 numbers are window-specific snapshots, not durable facts; the durable fact (no CPU limit ⇒ no throttle) is solid.

The request-vs-limit distinction is correct: a 4Gi limit does not consume schedulable capacity (only the 512Mi request does), so the claim that the limit blocked scale-out 'indirectly' (via node memory pressure / not via the scheduler) is the accurate framing, which the digest states. 1.25Gi = 1280Mi confirmed.

items.title: Dominant CI build cost is emulated arm64 (QEMU) → amd64-default + gated QEMU (ADR-0036)
items.claim: The shared build composite defaulted to platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64; homelab is amd64-only so arm64 was built under QEMU for nothing. ADR-0036 defaults to linux/amd64 and runs setup-qemu-action only if inputs.platforms != 'linux/amd64'. A caller passing explicit linux/amd64,linux/arm64 defeats the gate (it sees the comma'd value and emulates).
items.repoEvidence: docs/techdocs/docs/adr/adr-0036-amd64-default-constrictor-build.md:8-11 (composite defaults amd64,arm64; cluster amd64-only Talos; 'every build emits an arm64 image under QEMU emulation, slower than the entire action-clone wall combined'), :30-32 (setup-qemu gated if: ${{ inputs.platforms != 'linux/amd64' }}), :37-38 (arm64 stays reachable by passing both platforms, which re-enables QEMU via the gate). rfc-ci-pipeline-performance.md:23-25 + :99-101 (same). The composite itself is in webgrip/workflows (cross-repo).
items.webEvidence: Docker forums + multiple sources (setup-qemu-action#22, zenn 'Escaping QEMU Hell 15→2 min') confirm QEMU arm64 emulation is 4-5x slower for compile-heavy work and routinely makes builds 15-20 min — supports 'biggest cost' as plausible (digest correctly flags it [ASSERTED], first fast run 10m43s not yet A/B-confirmed).
items.reasoning: The gate-defeat subtlety is correct: a string equality test inputs.platforms != 'linux/amd64' is true for 'linux/amd64,linux/arm64', so QEMU runs — the caller must ALSO set platforms to pure linux/amd64. 'Biggest cost' is an assertion, not measured here, but the digest already marks it ASSERTED and the magnitude is well-supported. No falsification.
items.docImpact: None — keep the ASSERTED flag on 'biggest cost'; the mechanism and gate-defeat caveat are durable.

items.title: forgejo-runner 12.10.2 has NO action offline mode at any layer (ADR-0035)
items.claim: code.forgejo.org/forgejo/runner:12.10.2 exposes no way to skip re-fetching cached actions: generate-config cache: is ONLY the actions/cache server; one-job exposes only --url/--uuid/--token-url/--wait/--fetch-interval/--handle/--label; exec has STRIPPED --action-offline-mode/--action-cache-path leaving only --default-actions-url. exec -n (dryrun) does NOT clone actions and rejects -P/--platform (use -i/--image). items.title: Constrictor (strangler) migration for the build-workflow chain
items.claim: Build call graph: app-repo job → per-registry wrapper (docker-build-and-push-harbor.yml) → engine (docker-build-and-push-registry.yml) → composite (docker-build-push-registry). To change behavior without a flag-day: create parallel -fast files at each layer, leave originals untouched, migrate callers one at a time by flipping uses:, delete old chain once unreferenced. Snippet: uses: webgrip/workflows/.forgejo/workflows/docker-build-and-push-harbor-fast.yml@main + platforms: linux/amd64.

In-repo corroboration: rfc-ci-pipeline-performance.md:89-95 + adr-0036:26-35 describe the additive -fast composite + -fast reusable workflow + one-caller-at-a-time constrictor migration with the same naming (docker-build-push-registry-fast, docker-build-and-push-registry-fast.yml).
items.webEvidence: none — the call-graph filenames are repo-internal to webgrip/workflows.

Cannot independently confirm the precise filenames from here, but nothing contradicts them and they are HIGH/[VERIFIED] from the source thread. Marked MEDIUM only because the load-bearing files are cross-repo.

items.title: buildx must stay even for amd64-only — registry cache export needs its driver
items.claim: docker/setup-buildx-action cannot be dropped: cache-to/cache-from type=registry requires buildx's docker-container driver (the default docker driver can't). Only setup-qemu-action is safe to skip for pure-amd64. Harbor :cache repo also needs docker/login-action to pull even for read-only cache-from. A multi-platform buildx --push build leaves no local image, so a follow-up docker push won't work — dual-publish via one build with multiple registry-qualified tags + logins.
items.repoEvidence: docs/techdocs/docs/adr/adr-0036-amd64-default-constrictor-build.md:13-16 + :56-57 ('the registry cache export requires buildx docker-container driver, so dropping buildx would silently disable layer caching'; 'Drop buildx too... would break cache-to: type=registry (needs the docker-container driver)'). The fast composite (cross-repo webgrip/workflows) keeps setup-buildx + cache block verbatim.
items.webEvidence: Docker docs (docs.docker.com/build/cache/backends/registry/ and /build/builders/drivers/docker-container/) confirm: the registry cache backend is NOT supported by the default docker driver; use docker-container (or enable the containerd image store). moby/buildkit#606 and buildx discussions corroborate. Multi-platform --push leaving no loadable local image is standard buildx behavior (you cannot --load a multi-arch manifest).
items.reasoning: Core claim CONFIRMED. One nuance the digest omits: the default docker driver CAN do registry cache IF the containerd image store is enabled (newer Docker) — but dind in this setup uses the classic store, so 'the default docker driver can't' holds in practice. The 'login needed even for read-only cache-from' is correct (pulling cache manifests from a private Harbor repo requires auth). The 'no local image after multi-platform --push' point is accurate.
items.docImpact: Optional: note the docker-container-driver requirement is bypassable only with the containerd image store, which this dind does not use — so the practical rule (keep buildx) is unchanged.

items.title: Harbor registry layer cache (:cache) already ships in the composite
items.claim: webgrip/workflows .../docker-build-push-registry/action.yml derives the cache tag from the first normalized tag, strips the tag, appends :cache, then cache-from + cache-to type=registry,ref=…:cache,mode=max,compression=zstd. For Harbor: harbor.webgrip.dev/webgrip/<image>:cache. The -fast variant inherits it verbatim. Runtime cache-hit effectiveness not yet confirmed. Each cache-to mode=max overwrites the cache tag and orphans the prior ~2.6 GiB manifest (GC reclaims it).
items.repoEvidence: none (cross-repo) — the composite action.yml is in webgrip/workflows. In-repo corroboration: adr-0036:14-16 ('a Harbor registry layer cache (cache-from/cache-to: type=registry,…:cache,mode=max) — both must stay'); :44-45 ('Harbor registry layer cache is inherited unchanged — verified effective (cache import → CACHED layers on rebuild) as part of this work'); rfc-ci-pipeline-performance.md:27-30 ('Harbor registry layer cache (cache-from/cache-to: type=registry,…:cache,mode=max,compression=zstd) lives in the docker-build-push-registry composite today').
items.webEvidence: Docker registry-cache docs confirm mode=max,compression=zstd is valid syntax and that cache-to overwrites the referenced cache tag (a new manifest replaces the old, orphaning prior layers for GC). The :cache-tag-derivation detail is composite-internal.
items.reasoning: The cache block, ref shape (registry/owner/image:cache), mode=max, and zstd are corroborated by both ADR-0036 and the RFC. The tag-derivation algorithm (strip tag, append :cache) is composite-internal and not re-verifiable here, but is a standard pattern. 'cache-hit effectiveness not yet confirmed' in the digest is slightly at odds with adr-0036:44-45 which says it WAS 'verified effective' — a mild internal tension, but the digest is appropriately conservative (the ADR is aspirational/forward-looking about that verification).
items.docImpact: Minor: ADR-0036 asserts the layer cache was 'verified effective (CACHED layers on rebuild)'; the digest hedges 'not yet confirmed'. Reconcile by treating runtime hit-rate as still-to-measure (the safer stance); don't claim confirmed effectiveness in docs without a real two-build trace.

items.title: Make verifyRelease a buildx build that READS the publish path's :cache (no cache-to)
items.claim: The ~9-min per-image release cost was an uncached docker build inside semantic-release's verifyReleaseCmd. Fix: env-gate so when BUILD_CACHE_REF is set, run docker buildx build … --cache-from type=registry,ref=<ref> --output=type=cacheonly; else plain docker build. Deliberately NO cache-to because verify is amd64-only while publish is amd64+arm64 into the SAME :cache ref (amd64-only cache-to would clobber arm64 layers). Verify reads 'one release behind'. Plain-build fallback keeps GitHub unchanged.
items.repoEvidence: none (cross-repo) — .releaserc.js lives in webgrip/workflows. No .releaserc* found locally.
items.webEvidence: Docker buildx docs confirm --output=type=cacheonly produces no image (cache-only export, valid for a verify-only build) and --cache-from type=registry is read-only. The reasoning that an amd64-only cache-to into a shared multi-arch :cache ref would overwrite/clobber the arm64 cache manifest is consistent with cache-to mode=max replacing the whole cache index. semantic-release @semantic-release/exec verifyReleaseCmd running before the tag is cut is standard plugin lifecycle.
items.reasoning: Logic is internally consistent and matches buildx semantics; the 9-min figure and 'reads one release behind' are in-thread measurements/design not present here. The 'no cache-to to avoid clobbering arm64' is a sound and non-obvious insight. Marked MEDIUM: cross-repo file + the speedup is explicitly ASSERTED-until-run by the digest.
items.docImpact: None — keep the ASSERTED flag on the cache-hit speedup; the cache-only/no-cache-to rationale is durable and correct.

items.title: @semantic-release/exec verifyReleaseCmd is Lodash-templated — build dynamic strings in JS
items.claim: semantic-release expands ${...} in exec commands as Lodash templates against its release context (${nextRelease.version}), NOT as shell — you cannot put ${process.env.FOO} in the command string. Since .releaserc.js is real JS, read process.env and interpolate at config-load time. successCmd: 'echo "version=${nextRelease.version}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT' is the intended Lodash use.
items.repoEvidence: none (cross-repo) — .releaserc.js is in webgrip/workflows.
items.webEvidence: The @semantic-release/exec plugin documents that command templates are expanded with Lodash template against the plugin context (branch, lastRelease, nextRelease, commits, releases) — it is Lodash, not shell, interpolation. So ${nextRelease.version} resolves but ${process.env.FOO} does not (process.env is not in the template context). Reading process.env in a .releaserc.js (a JS module) at load time is the correct pattern. (General semantic-release/exec behavior; not version-pinned here.)
items.reasoning: Accurate. One precision: $GITHUB_OUTPUT in the successCmd is a literal shell var passed through to the spawned shell (Lodash only touches ${...}, and $GITHUB_OUTPUT has no braces), so that part is shell, while ${nextRelease.version} is Lodash — the digest's example correctly shows both. Digest marks this MEDIUM/[ASSERTED]; the behavior is well-established, so it could arguably be HIGH, but MEDIUM is safe given no in-repo file to confirm the exact config.
items.docImpact: None — solid gotcha; could upgrade confidence to HIGH given it is documented @semantic-release/exec behavior.

items.title: Base-image pull mirroring belongs in the buildx builder's buildkitd config, not a dind ConfigMap
items.claim: Routing docker build FROM pulls through Harbor by mounting daemon.json/buildkitd.toml into the dind sidecar is the wrong layer: daemon.json registry-mirrors only mirrors Docker Hub (classic/embedded driver); buildkitd.toml is read by a standalone buildkitd — i.e. only a docker-container-driver builder, whose --config is resolved from the RUNNER container's filesystem, not dind's. The node mirror only accelerates kubelet/containerd pod-image pulls; the dind daemon does NOT use the node mirror for build-time pulls. So per-registry base-image mirrors must live in that builder's buildkitd config, configured in webgrip/workflows. (Shipped alternative: parameterized base-registry ARGs.)
items.repoEvidence: none (cross-repo) — the buildx/dind builder config lives in webgrip/workflows. Corroborated by user memory note 'Forgejo runner base-image mirror layer' (dind-ConfigMap dead-end backed out 2026-06-25; mirroring belongs in the buildx docker-container builder config).
items.webEvidence: Confirmed by primary sources: dockerd daemon.json registry-mirrors mirrors ONLY Docker Hub (Docker docs / forums — does not mirror arbitrary registries); buildkitd.toml supports per-registry [registry."host"] mirrors and is the buildx/BuildKit mechanism (docs.docker.com/build/buildkit/configure/); moby/buildkit#606 confirms BuildKit does NOT respect daemon.json registry mirrors. So the layering argument is correct: daemon.json won't help non-DockerHub bases, and buildkitd.toml is consumed by the docker-container builder (configured via --buildkitd-config from the runner/host filesystem), not by dind's dockerd.
items.reasoning: Every technical sub-claim is upstream-verified. One refinement: the buildkitd.toml --config is passed to `docker buildx create --buildkitd-config <path>`, and that path is read by the buildx CLI from wherever the CLI runs (the runner container), then injected into the spawned buildkitd container — so 'resolved from the runner container's filesystem' is correct. The node mirror serving only containerd/kubelet (not dind's separate dockerd engine) is also correct. Digest marks MEDIUM/[ASSERTED] (design analysis, approach built then backed out) — appropriate.
items.docImpact: None — the layering analysis is upstream-correct; keep the conclusion that base-registry mirroring belongs in the buildx builder's buildkitd config (or via base-registry ARGs), not a dind daemon.json/ConfigMap.

summary: Verified all 10 knowledge items in B3-dockerfile-and-semrel.md. items.title: semantic-release on Forgejo: @saithodev/semantic-release-gitea, env-gated on a LITERAL flag
items.claim: On Forgejo the GitHub plugin's success step POSTs /api/v1/graphql (getAssociatedPRs) → 404 → exit 1 (tag+Release already created). Shared .releaserc.js selects the publish plugin at require-time, gated on a LITERAL SEMANTIC_RELEASE_GITEA='true' exported by the action, NOT on GITEA_URL derived from vars.FORGEJO_INSTANCE_URL (which was unset → GITEA_URL='' → silent fallback to GitHub). Let GITEA_URL fall back to github.server_url. Also: npx semantic-release --config <file> silently ignores --config (cosmiconfig resolves from cwd, so each image's ops/docker/<image>/.releaserc.cjs wins).

(https://github.com/semantic-release/semantic-release/discussions/3163, https://semantic-release.gitbook.io/semantic-release/usage/configuration)

items.title: Architecture: 'release once, publish many' — Forgejo sole authority, GitHub pure mirror + version commit-back
items.claim: Exactly one system decides a version (semantic-release/tags/changelog/package.json bump). Forgejo-gated @semantic-release/npm (npmPublish:false) + @semantic-release/git commit-back (chore(release): ${nextRelease.gitTag} [skip ci], identity webgrip-ci <ci@webgrip.nl>); both forges honor [skip ci].

summary: Verified all 4 items in B4-signing-verify-policy.md against the repo (Kyverno ClusterPolicies, OpenBao bootstrap scripts, cosign-pubkey CronJob, runbook) and upstream cosign/Kyverno docs+issues. Verdicts: 2 PARTIAL, 1 CONFIRMED, 1 PARTIAL. The signing/authorization architecture (OpenBao Transit ECDSA-P256 key never leaving the vault, Forgejo OIDC→JWT auth role with the workflow_dispatch claim shape, audience openbao-cosign, pubkey-publishing CronJob, break-glass generate-root, --tlog-upload=false being necessary since cosign 2.0 uploads key-based sigs to Rekor by default) is solidly CONFIRMED. Two recurring claims are now WRONG: (1) "GHCR is keyless OIDC/Fulcio/Rekor" / "the GHCR keyless policy explicitly sets rekor.url" — as of commit fc5fdf1 (2026-06-26) webgrip GHCR images were flipped to KEY-based signing with the same OpenBao Transit key as Harbor; the only keyless+rekor.url rule left is for THIRD-PARTY ghcr.io/kyverno/* images, not webgrip. (2) "no rekor block → no tlog lookup" is REFUTED by upstream: Kyverno (Cosign 2.0 integration, this repo runs 1.18.1) verifies the Rekor tlog entry for key-based signing BY DEFAULT; omitting the rekor block does NOT disable it — you must set rekor.ignoreTlog:true (issue #11771 is this exact --tlog-upload=false scenario failing). The repo's three verify policies omit both the rekor block AND ignoreTlog, so they are a latent mis-config that only hasn't fired because they're Audit-only and never exercised against a real signed image (the CLI test only asserts skip).

items.title: cosign signing via OpenBao Transit, authorized by Forgejo Actions OIDC, key-only Kyverno verify (no Rekor)
items.claim: Harbor AND GHCR images are key-signed (cosign --tlog-upload=false --key hashivault://cosign-webgrip, OpenBao Transit ECDSA-P256, key never leaves OpenBao); Forgejo OIDC (iss https://forgejo.webgrip.dev/api/actions) + auth/forgejo JWT role cosign-signer mints a sign-only token; pubkey published to cosign-webgrip-pub ConfigMap by a CronJob; Kyverno image-verify-harbor-audit verifies key-only with no rekor block so no tlog lookup, while the GHCR keyless policy explicitly sets rekor.url; --tlog-upload=false needed; one-time generate-root break-glass enables Transit+forgejo jwt; sign-attest signs BY DIGEST and is generalized to REGISTRY_USERNAME/REGISTRY_TOKEN for both registries.
items.repoEvidence: kubernetes/apps/security/openbao/bootstrap/init.sh:45-46 (secrets enable transit; transit/keys/cosign-webgrip type=ecdsa-p256; lines 40-44 call out the break-glass generate-root); kubernetes/apps/security/openbao/bootstrap/cosign-signer.hcl:6-15 (sign-only on transit/sign/cosign-webgrip + read transit/keys/cosign-webgrip); config.sh:58-84 (auth/forgejo oidc_discovery_url https://forgejo.${SECRET_DOMAIN}/api/actions, role cosign-signer, audience openbao-cosign); kubernetes/apps/security/cosign-pubkey/app/publish.cronjob.yaml:1-69 (CronJob mirrors Transit pubkey to cosign-webgrip-pub ConfigMap); image-verify-harbor-audit.yaml:62-63 (keys.publicKeys, NO rekor block). CONTRADICTS the digest: image-verify-audit.yaml:48-55 webgrip GHCR is now KEY-based (keys.publicKeys, no rekor), and the ONLY rekor.url is on the third-party kyverno rule at lines 87-88. The sign-attest action + REGISTRY_USERNAME/TOKEN generalization + imagetools digest resolution live in webgrip/infrastructure: none (cross-repo).
items.webEvidence: https://github.com/sigstore/cosign (CHANGELOG/2.0 blog): cosign 2.0 uploads signatures to Rekor by default for BOTH key-based and identity-based signing, so --tlog-upload=false is genuinely required even with --key hashivault — confirms that part. https://main.kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/cluster-policy/verify-images/sigstore/ and issue #11771: Kyverno verifies the Rekor tlog entry for key-based signing by DEFAULT; omitting the rekor block does NOT skip the tlog — you must set rekor.ignoreTlog:true. So 'no rekor block -> no tlog lookup' is not how Kyverno works.
items.reasoning: Architecture half (Transit/ECDSA-P256/JWT/issuer/CronJob/break-glass/--tlog-upload=false) is fully confirmed in-repo and by upstream. Two embedded claims fail falsification: (a) 'GHCR is keyless / the GHCR keyless policy explicitly sets rekor.url' is now STALE — commit fc5fdf1 (2026-06-26) rewrote image-verify-audit.yaml to key-sign webgrip GHCR with the SAME Transit key, removing the keyless+rekor.url attestor; rekor.url now only governs third-party kyverno images. (b) 'no rekor block -> no tlog lookup' is contradicted by upstream Kyverno behavior (default tlog verification on). The repo policies omit both the rekor block and ignoreTlog, so they would attempt a tlog lookup and FAIL to verify a --tlog-upload=false signature — a latent bug masked by Audit mode (failurePolicy Ignore/Fail) and the fact the CLI test only asserts 'skip', never real verification.
items.correction: Harbor AND webgrip GHCR are both key-signed with the one OpenBao Transit ECDSA-P256 key (cosign-webgrip), --tlog-upload=false, key never leaving OpenBao; authz via Forgejo OIDC -> auth/forgejo JWT role cosign-signer (audience openbao-cosign); pubkey mirrored to the cosign-webgrip-pub ConfigMap by a CronJob; Transit+forgejo-jwt enabled by a one-time generate-root break-glass. BUT: there is no 'GHCR keyless policy with rekor.url' for webgrip images anymore — rekor.url only appears on the third-party ghcr.io/kyverno/* rule. AND key-only Kyverno verification does NOT skip the Rekor tlog just because the rekor block is omitted — Kyverno (1.18.1, cosign 2.0) verifies the tlog by default; to actually verify a --tlog-upload=false signature each verifyImages entry needs an explicit rekor: {ignoreTlog: true} (the repo policies currently lack it).
items.docImpact: Yes, material. (1) Stop describing webgrip GHCR as keyless OIDC/Fulcio/Rekor — it is key-based as of 2026-06-26 (same key as Harbor); reserve 'keyless + rekor.url' language for third-party kyverno images. (2) Correct the 'no rekor block -> no tlog lookup' assertion that is repeated verbatim in image-verify-audit.yaml:49-50, image-attestations-audit.yaml:46-48, image-verify-harbor-audit.yaml and the digest: Kyverno verifies the tlog by default; --tlog-upload=false signatures need an explicit rekor.ignoreTlog:true. Flag the three verify policies as a pending fix before any Audit->Enforce promotion, and note the stale supply-chain-pipeline.md (still says 'keyless') and the stale CLI test rule names (verify-github-slsa-provenance/verify-cyclonedx-sbom no longer exist).

items.title: Harbor SBOM trigger lives only in the Forgejo action; the two cosign actions are NOT symmetric
items.claim: Harbor-native-SBOM step exists ONLY in .forgejo/actions/cosign-sign-attest (if: contains(inputs.registry,'harbor')); GHCR has no server-side SBOM API; Forgejo/Harbor signs key-based via OpenBao Transit (--tlog-upload=false) while the .github mirror pushes to GHCR via keyless OIDC/Fulcio/Rekor; SBOM step is fail-soft (::warning:: on non-2xx); tokens via a 0600 curl -K config file not -u (literal $/+ in robot$webgrip+ci survive in quoted user=); installs cosign v2.4.3 + Syft v1.21.0 pinned to github.com (data.forgejo.org 404s); CycloneDX+SPDX; OpenBao role cosign-signer, JWT path /v1/auth/forgejo/login, audience openbao-cosign; DT http://dependency-track-api-server.security.svc.cluster.local:8080/api/v1/bom.
items.repoEvidence: DT endpoint host:port confirmed: kubernetes/apps/security/dependency-track/app/sbom-uploader/cronjob.yaml:58 and policy-bootstrap/job.yaml:27 both use http://dependency-track-api-server.security.svc.cluster.local:8080. OpenBao role cosign-signer + auth/forgejo + audience openbao-cosign confirmed in config.sh:65-75. harbor-proxy-config.configmap.yaml:116 references the Harbor SBOM 403/empty-column behavior (consistent with the SBOM step). The cosign-sign-attest action itself (registry gate, curl -K 0600, v2.4.3/v1.21.0 pins, ::warning:: fail-soft, /v1/auth/forgejo/login path, .github keyless mirror) lives in webgrip/infrastructure + webgrip/workflows: none (cross-repo).
items.webEvidence: cosign v2.4.3 and Syft v1.21.0 are real released versions; data.forgejo.org being an unreliable action host (forcing absolute github.com pins) is a known Forgejo Actions limitation. The '.github mirror is keyless GHCR' assertion is the now-stale model — see item 1: webgrip GHCR Kyverno verification was flipped to key-based 2026-06-26.
items.reasoning: Most of this describes the cross-repo action and can't be byte-verified here, but every in-repo touchpoint (DT URL+port+path shape, OpenBao role/path/audience, Harbor SBOM 403 behavior) checks out and the reasoning (GHCR has no server-side SBOM API so the Harbor SBOM step is meaningless there; -K keeps the robot token out of the process list; literal $/+ survive inside a quoted user=) is sound. The one falsifiable defect: 'the .github mirror pushes to GHCR via keyless OIDC/Fulcio/Rekor' conflates the BUILD/PUSH signing on the .github side with the cluster's Kyverno VERIFICATION model, which is now key-based for webgrip GHCR. Whether the .github action still signs keyless at push time is cross-repo and unconfirmed; regardless the cluster no longer verifies webgrip GHCR keyless.
items.correction: Keep the SBOM-asymmetry, fail-soft, curl -K 0600, version-pin and DT-endpoint facts. Correct the framing that GHCR is 'keyless': the cluster verifies webgrip GHCR with the OpenBao Transit KEY now (item 1). If the .github mirror still signs keyless at push time, that is build-side only and is no longer what Kyverno trusts — so do not present GHCR as a keyless target in the trust chain.
items.docImpact: Mostly keep; one fix. Preserve 'SBOM step is Harbor-only, fail-soft; tokens via 0600 curl -K not -u; cosign 2.4.3/Syft 1.21.0 pinned to github.com; DT at :8080/api/v1/bom' as durable CI gotchas. Drop/qualify the 'GHCR = keyless Fulcio/Rekor' characterization to avoid contradicting the current key-based Kyverno policies. Note the cross-repo files (.forgejo/actions/cosign-sign-attest) are not in this repo and should be verified there before treating the action-internal details as ground truth.

items.title: Kyverno policies are Audit-only; Harbor Deployment security stays OFF
items.claim: Kyverno policies image-verify-audit, image-attestations-audit, image-verify-harbor-audit are all validationFailureAction: Audit (flip to Enforce only once green with zero false positives, explicitly NOT done); leave Harbor project Deployment-security OFF because Harbor's cosign check only verifies a signature exists (not against your key) and blocks pulls of unsigned artifacts incl the buildx :cache tag (breaking cache-from), and 'Prevent vulnerable images' can make a running image unpullable on a new CVE; enforce only at Kyverno admission.
items.repoEvidence: All three policies confirmed validationFailureAction: Audit — image-verify-audit.yaml:22, image-attestations-audit.yaml:20, image-verify-harbor-audit.yaml:24. 'Not yet Enforce' confirmed by policy comments (image-verify-harbor-audit.yaml:27-32 'Flip to Fail when promoting... to Enforce') and image-attestations-audit.yaml:19 'flip to Enforce once green'. The Harbor Deployment-security toggle is a Harbor UI/project setting with NO manifest in this repo (grep of kubernetes/apps/harbor found no deployment_security/content_trust/notary keys): none (in this repo).
items.webEvidence: Harbor docs/general knowledge: Harbor's cosign 'deployment security' / signature gate verifies that a signature EXISTS (presence), not that it matches your specific public key, and 'Prevent vulnerable images from running' blocks pulls when a scan finds CVEs >= a severity. Both are accurate descriptions of Harbor behavior. The buildx :cache-tag-gets-blocked failure mode is a plausible consequence (a cache manifest is an unsigned artifact) but I found no single primary source stating it verbatim.
items.reasoning: Audit-only half is fully verified in-repo and is the load-bearing, actionable part. The Harbor-OFF half is, as the digest itself flags, single-source ASSERTED: it is a Harbor server-side config decision not represented in any manifest here, so it cannot be repo-verified. The technical rationale (presence-only signature check; CVE-gate can strand a running image; cache tag is unsigned) is individually plausible and consistent with Harbor's documented behavior, but the specific 'breaks cache-from' interaction is reasoned, not cited. Verdict PARTIAL: Audit-only CONFIRMED, Harbor-OFF UNVERIFIABLE-in-repo-but-plausible.
items.correction: The three Kyverno policies are Audit-only and explicitly not yet promoted to Enforce (confirmed). The 'keep Harbor Deployment-security OFF' guidance is a sound but in-repo-unverifiable operational decision (Harbor UI config, no manifest); its rationale (Harbor's cosign gate only checks signature presence not your key; a CVE gate can make a running image unpullable; the unsigned buildx :cache tag would be blocked, breaking cache-from) is plausible and consistent with Harbor docs but not primary-sourced for the cache-from interaction specifically. Enforce signature/key trust only at Kyverno admission.
items.docImpact: Keep both, with a confidence note. Record 'all image-verify policies Audit-only; do NOT flip to Enforce until a real signed release verifies green AND the policies gain rekor.ignoreTlog:true (see item 1)' as the promotion gate. Keep the Harbor-Deployment-security-OFF rationale in a runbook/ADR but tag it ASSERTED/operational (no manifest backs it); if it is to be durable guidance, capture the exact Harbor project setting and the cache-tag failure mode from a Harbor primary source.

It does branch: ScanTypeSbom forces res=ResourceSBOM, so a robot with only scan:create (let alone only repository push/pull) gets 403. The .sbom-vs-.att distinction holds: a cyclonedx cosign attestation is a .att accessory (Signed), distinct media type from Harbor's native .sbom. Version-specificity (>=2.11) is real and matches the deployed v2.15.1. No contradiction found.

items.title: Talos registry mirror is a silent no-op unless nodes can DNS-resolve the Harbor hostname
items.claim: Mirror endpoints point at https://harbor.${secretDomain}/v2/...; nodes resolve via 1.1.1.1/1.0.0.1 and harbor.webgrip.dev is LAN-only -> NXDOMAIN -> containerd silently falls back to upstream; image pulls use node DNS not pod/cluster DNS; fix = static Talos extraHostEntries mapping harbor host -> 10.0.0.27 (envoy-internal LAN VIP).

(No single primary source asserts every sub-clause verbatim.)
items.reasoning: Core mechanism (manifest-GET != registered/cached/scannable; digest-pin == byte-identity) is sound and matches Harbor/OCI behavior, but the item is largely a not-yet-built procedure (skopeo Job, ref-normalization heuristics, sed rewrite) with no committed code to verify, and the digest itself flags 'full warm run pending'. So it cannot be marked fully CONFIRMED — it is correct-but-unexecuted. items.correction: Mechanism is correct (manifest GET does not register/cache/scan; a real pull does; digest pin already guarantees image==running without forcing pulls). The skopeo-Job warm procedure and ref-normalization rules are a documented design that has not yet been built or run in-cluster ('full warm run pending'); treat them as a plan, not a verified procedure.
items.docImpact: When writing docs/skills, present the manifest-GET-vs-pull mechanism and the digest-pin equivalence as established, but mark the skopeo warm-Job (and the tag+digest / docker.io normalization details) as an unexecuted procedure to validate before relying on it.

summary: Verified all 9 items in B6 against repo evidence (the forgejo-leading SKILL.md, scripts/forgejo-sync.sh, gitea-mirror helmrelease/configmap, git log) and Forgejo/GitHub primary sources. Verdicts: 6 CONFIRMED, 3 PARTIAL. The procedural core (cutover order, Danger-Zone "Convert to a regular repository", workflow-scope PAT requirement, Forgejo token-scope 403 behavior, status-check-context deadlock, gitea-mirror SQLite store, forgejo-sync.sh shapes, gh api stdout-on-404) is all well-supported by both repo and primary sources. The three PARTIALs are accuracy nuances, not refutations: (1) the "un-mirror has NO REST API / UI-only" assertion is now OUTDATED — Forgejo PR #8932 added POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/convert (mirror→regular) merged 2025-09-14 and backported to 15.0/16.0; the cluster runs forgejo-helm chart 17.1.0 → app ~15.0.x, which plausibly has it, so the UI-click is no longer the ONLY way; (2) the digest's "Verified on Forgejo 15.0.2" is an approximation — the chart pins 17.1.0 (chart, not app) and the current chart main appVersion is 15.0.3, so the family is right but the exact patch isn't pinned in-repo; (3) ".profile-private" for members-only org profile is a GitHub convention not documented by Forgejo (only the public .profile repo with root README.md is). None of the items are REFUTED.

items.title: Forgejo-leading repo cutover (order is load-bearing; un-mirror is Danger-Zone "Convert")
items.claim: Per-repo cutover order: (1) disable repo in gitea-mirror UI, (2) Forgejo Synchronize-now then Danger Zone -> 'Convert to a regular repository' (flips is_mirror=false; the Mirror-settings panel has no un-mirror button), (3) re-point remotes. gitea-mirror disable alone does NOT stop Forgejo's scheduled pull; the convert does. Two shapes: convert existing pull-mirror vs create-empty-and-push.
items.repoEvidence: .claude/skills/forgejo-leading/SKILL.md:16-39 ("Order is load-bearing (mirror-clobber race)"; Danger Zone -> Convert to a regular repository; "The Mirror-settings panel has no un-mirror button (only Synchronize / interval / prune)"; "Verified on Forgejo 15.0.2 (gitea-1.22)") and :25-27 (the 404 create-empty shape). Re-point snippet matches SKILL.md:42-53.
items.webEvidence: https://forgejo.org/docs/v15.0/user/repo-mirror/ confirms Synchronize-Now exists and that pull-mirroring is one-directional ("Once the repository is created, you can't convert it into a pull mirror anymore"); WebSearch result on the danger-zone convert: "a feature ... to change a mirror repository into a regular repository, accessible in the danger zone of the repository settings" and the .mirror boolean. https://forgejo.org/docs/latest/user/profile/ independently confirms the danger-zone convert pattern for repos.
items.reasoning: The ORDER and the Danger-Zone-Convert mechanism are solidly confirmed. The one falsifiable sub-claim is "the Mirror-settings panel has NO un-mirror button (UI-only)": Forgejo PR #8932 added a programmatic POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/convert (merged 2025-09-14, backported to 15.0/16.0). So as of recent 15.0.x the un-mirror is NOT strictly UI-only. The cluster runs forgejo-helm 17.1.0 (app ~15.0.x) which plausibly carries the backport. The panel still likely lacks a button (the convert is in Danger Zone, not the mirror panel) so that specific UI claim holds; but the broader skill framing that there is 'no REST API' for un-mirroring is outdated.
items.correction: The cutover order and Danger-Zone 'Convert to a regular repository' are correct. Caveat: un-mirroring is no longer strictly UI-only — Forgejo added POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/convert (PR #8932, merged 2025-09-14, backported to 15.0/16.0), so on the cluster's recent Forgejo it can also be scripted via the API. The convert still lives in Danger Zone, not the mirror-settings panel.
items.docImpact: Update the skill's 'no REST API' framing: note that recent Forgejo (>=15.0 backport) exposes POST /api/v1/repos/{o}/{r}/convert, which COULD let the un-mirror step be automated for bulk migrations (the digest's own motivation 'tens to hundreds of repos'). Keep the manual Danger-Zone path as the documented/verified route but stop asserting it's the ONLY way.

items.title: Verify un-mirror via the .mirror flag, NOT anon permissions.push
items.claim: GET /api/v1/repos/<o>/<r> permissions.push reflects the anonymous caller (reads false even for converted writable repos); the reliable signal is the top-level .mirror boolean (or a successful git push).

Confirms .mirror is the authoritative top-level field. permissions.push reflecting the (unauthenticated) caller is standard Gitea/Forgejo API behavior for anonymous requests.
items.reasoning: Two independent factual claims: (a) .mirror is the reliable field — directly confirmed by Forgejo API behavior; (b) permissions.push is caller-relative — an unauthenticated curl has no push perm, so it returns false regardless of repo writability. Both hold; nothing version-specific. The advice is sound.
items.docImpact: None — claim stands as written.

items.title: GitHub push-mirror PAT needs the workflow scope, not just repo
items.claim: A Forgejo->GitHub push-mirror pushes all refs; GitHub rejects any ref touching .github/workflows/ unless the PAT has the workflow scope. Tags/releases sync (no workflow files) but a commit changing a workflow file is rejected -> 'release mirrors but code doesn't'. Fix: add workflow scope to the existing classic PAT.
items.repoEvidence: none (cross-repo) — the push-mirror PAT (GH_MIRROR_TOKEN) lives in ~/.config/webgrip/forgejo.env, not in-repo. scripts/forgejo-sync.sh:24 documents GH_MIRROR_TOKEN as 'GitHub PAT (classic, scope repo)' but does NOT mention the workflow scope, which is consistent with this being a discovered gotcha.
items.webEvidence: GitHub docs + community discussion #26254 / johnnycase blog confirm the exact error: 'refusing to allow a Personal Access Token to create or update workflow .github/workflows/<file> without workflow scope', and the fix is adding the workflow scope to the classic PAT. Matches the digest snippet verbatim in form.
items.reasoning: Tried to break it: could releases-sync-but-code-doesn't have another cause? The mechanism is precise — GitHub's ref-update guard is specifically on workflow-file paths, so tags (which don't modify workflow files) pass while a commit editing a workflow is rejected, leaving the branch behind. The 'add workflow to existing classic PAT (same value -> stored creds gain scope)' part is true because Forgejo stores the token string, and re-scoping a classic PAT keeps the same token value. Solid.
items.docImpact: Worth adding to scripts/forgejo-sync.sh's GH_MIRROR_TOKEN comment: scope must be 'repo + workflow' (not just 'repo'), else workflow-file commits fail to mirror while tags/releases still sync. Currently the script comment says only 'scope repo'.

items.title: Converting a pull-mirror leaves Actions AND Pull-Requests units OFF; library repos keep Actions OFF
items.claim: gitea-mirror creates the Forgejo repo with has_actions=false and converting leaves has_pull_requests=false; PRs-off makes Renovate skip ('pull requests are disabled'). Re-enable via PATCH /repos/{o}/{r}. EXCEPTION: reusable-workflow library repos (webgrip/workflows) keep Actions OFF (ACTIONS_OFF_REPOS="workflows"; commit 'feat(forgejo): force Actions unit OFF for reusable-workflow library repos').
items.repoEvidence: scripts/forgejo-sync.sh:6-9 (actions/prs unit logic), :42-46 (ACTIONS_OFF_REPOS="workflows" + the workflow_call rationale), :89-117 (sync_actions forces has_actions=false for off-repos; sync_prs PATCHes has_pull_requests=true with comment 'un-mirror leaves it off; Renovate skips PR-less repos'). git log confirms commit 37b0fce 'feat(forgejo): force Actions unit OFF for reusable-workflow library repos' (also visible in the harness's recent-commits list).
items.webEvidence: Renovate's 'pull requests are disabled' skip behavior is standard Renovate-on-Gitea/Forgejo behavior; the workflow_call reusable-workflow model (runs in caller's runner, uses: resolves without the unit being on) is standard Forgejo Actions behavior. No primary-source contradiction found.
items.reasoning: Every factual sub-claim has a direct code anchor. The exception's rationale (disabling the Actions unit doesn't break uses: consumers because the reusable workflow executes in the caller) is the documented Forgejo/GitHub-Actions semantics for on: workflow_call. The commit message matches exactly. Nothing to falsify.
items.docImpact: None — fully supported by code + commit.

items.title: Don't copy GitHub status_check_contexts into Forgejo branch protection
items.claim: Copying GitHub's required_status_checks.contexts (e.g. 'validate (default.json)') deadlocks every Forgejo merge because those are GitHub job names Forgejo never reports. Keep only structural rules (require PR+approvals, block force-push/deletion); re-add status checks later under actual Forgejo check names.
items.repoEvidence: scripts/forgejo-sync.sh:152-165 — sync_protect explicitly does NOT copy status_check_contexts ('those are GitHub job names and would deadlock Forgejo merges (Forgejo checks are named differently)'), builds only rule_name=main with enable_push and optional required_approvals, and emits a note when GitHub has required_status_checks that they are 'NOT copied ... re-add under Forgejo check names once CI has run'. Baseline/strict bodies match the digest snippet ({"rule_name":"main","enable_push":true} vs strict with required_approvals).
items.webEvidence: Forgejo branch-protection 'required status checks' match against patterns of the commit-status contexts produced by that Forgejo instance's CI; GitHub's context names (job names) are never emitted by Forgejo, so a required check that is never reported blocks merge indefinitely. Consistent with Forgejo branch-protection docs behavior.
items.reasoning: Code is the spec here and it matches the claim exactly, including the warning text and the baseline-vs-strict rule shapes. The deadlock mechanism (a required status that is never reported can't be satisfied) is logically and behaviorally sound for both Forgejo and GitHub. No counter-evidence.
items.docImpact: None — code-anchored and correct.

items.title: Forgejo PAT granular scopes — /user 403s, org-list needs read:organization
items.claim: A Forgejo PAT scoped only write:repository 403s on GET /api/v1/user (needs read:user); validate via a repo endpoint instead. GET /orgs/{org}/repos (for --all sweeps) needs read:organization. A 403 (not 401) = valid token, missing scope.

items.webEvidence: https://forgejo.org/docs/latest/user/token-scope/ confirms: read:user 'Grants read access to user operations'; read:organization grants 'read access to org and team status, such as listing all organizations'; write:repository 'Grants read/write/delete access to repository operations'; and '(no scope) Not supported. A scope is required even for public repositories.' Forgejo returns 403 (forbidden, authenticated-but-out-of-scope) vs 401 (unauthenticated), matching the claim.

The 403-vs-401 distinction is standard HTTP/Forgejo semantics (valid token, insufficient scope = 403). No version sensitivity in the scope model.

items.title: gh api prints error body to stdout on 404 — fallback must be outside $(...)
items.claim: On HTTP error, gh api writes the error JSON to stdout and exits non-zero. So x=$(gh api ... || echo '{}') concatenates error JSON with {} -> a 'two JSON docs' parse crash. Put the fallback outside the substitution: ghp=$(gh api ... 2>/dev/null) || ghp='{}'.
items.repoEvidence: scripts/forgejo-sync.sh:149-151 — the exact pattern with an inline code comment: 'NB: gh prints the error body to stdout on 404, so keep the fallback OUTSIDE the command substitution — || echo {} inside would concatenate two JSON docs.' followed by `local ghp; ghp=$(gh api "repos/$ORG/$r/branches/main/protection" 2>/dev/null) || ghp='{}'`.
items.webEvidence: cli/cli issue #5209 ('gh api: give the user control over HTTP error responses') and community discussion #47013 confirm gh api emits the JSON error body to stdout and exits non-zero on HTTP errors (e.g. 404 returns the {"message":"Not Found",...} body to stdout plus a non-zero exit). This makes `$(gh api ... || echo '{}')` concatenate the error JSON with {}, breaking a downstream JSON parse — exactly as claimed.
items.reasoning: The behavior is confirmed by the gh source/issue tracker AND demonstrated in this very repo's script with the same reasoning. The shell-semantics claim (|| inside $() runs only after capture, so error stdout is already captured and then '{}' is appended) is correct: command substitution captures stdout regardless of exit code, and `cmd || echo {}` appends on failure. Moving the fallback outside ($(...) 2>/dev/null) || var='{}' makes the failure path discard stdout entirely. Airtight.
items.docImpact: None — confirmed by both the in-repo script comment and gh upstream behavior.
