items.title: task talos:upgrade-node built-in drain STALLS on single-replica-PDB workloads — force-drain first
items.claim: talosctl upgrade's internal cordon+drain cannot evict single-replica workloads with PDBs (allowedDisruptions:0) (kyverno background/reports controllers + single-instance CNPG DBs), hits a 5m timeout, aborts, and leaves the node staged-but-not-rebooted; fix is kubectl drain --disable-eviction first, then task talos:upgrade-node.
items.repoEvidence: .taskfiles/talos/Taskfile.yaml upgrade-node uses talosImageURL/talosVersion lookup (lines ~60-75); kubernetes/apps/kyverno/kyverno/app/helmrelease.yaml:122-135 backgroundController replicas:1 + podDisruptionBudget minAvailable:1, lines 161-195 reportsController replicas:1 + minAvailable:1; ~10-15 CNPG Clusters with instances:1 (e.g. kubernetes/apps/security/dependency-track/app/database/cluster.yaml:8) on storageClass: longhorn (cluster.yaml:24).
items.webEvidence: github.com/siderolabs/talos/issues/9882 'Talos operations that drain nodes (upgrade, shutdown, etc) do not respect podDisruptionBudget' confirms the talosctl-internal-drain-vs-PDB tension; kubernetes.io disruptions docs + drain docs confirm a minAvailable:1 PDB on a single-replica deployment yields ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS 0 and blocks eviction, and that --disable-eviction forces delete (bypasses PDBs). The 5m default eviction/drain window matches reported talosctl behavior.
items.reasoning: Mechanism is upstream-documented and repo state matches (single-replica PDBs exist on the named controllers and on every single-instance CNPG cluster). Caveat: the current kyverno PDBs carry unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy: AlwaysAllow, but that only frees UNHEALTHY pods — a healthy single-replica pod with allowedDisruptions:0 still blocks the internal drain, so the gotcha and the force-drain fix remain valid. The '5m global timeout' is a talosctl default, not the recipe's --timeout=10m flag, so no contradiction.
items.docImpact: None — keep as written. Optionally note the recipe's own --timeout is 10m while the internal eviction loop is the 5m one, to avoid confusion.

items.title: etcd quorum / HA — corrects the 'go to 1 control-plane' intuition
items.claim: etcd quorum = floor(N/2)+1, tolerated failures = N-quorum (3→1, 5→2), odd counts only; 3 CP is the HA minimum, dropping to 1 is strictly less resilient; no automated etcd backup yet (roadmap #52); the real soyo fragility is correlated failure + etcd fsync sensitivity under Longhorn, not node count.
items.repoEvidence: docs/techdocs/docs/general/roadmap.md:25 'no off-node etcd snapshot backup', :155 item 52 'etcd off-node snapshot backup → Garage S3 (no automated etcd backup exists)', :252 '#52 etcd backup is the missing leg'; no etcd backup config found under kubernetes/ or talos/. ADR-0026 documents the etcd-fsync-vs-Longhorn correlated-failure rationale (single 512GB SSD shared by etcd/OS/Longhorn). Memory tenant-db-on-etcd-node-leader-change.md corroborates the leader-election event.
items.webEvidence: etcd.io FAQ + multiple references confirm majority=(n/2)+1, fault tolerance = n−quorum, 3→tolerates 1, 5→tolerates 2, and odd-number recommendation (an odd cluster tolerates the same failures as the next-larger even one with fewer nodes).
items.reasoning: Quorum math is textbook-correct and matches primary etcd docs exactly. The 'no automated etcd backup' status is confirmed by the roadmap and the absence of any etcd-snapshot manifest. The correlated-failure framing is the accepted ADR-0026 rationale. Failover wasn't exercised in-thread (digest already flags this).
items.docImpact: None.

items.title: Break a goharbor RWO RollingUpdate Multi-Attach deadlock by deleting the old ReplicaSet
items.claim: The goharbor chart hardcodes RollingUpdate (renders both strategy blocks so Recreate-via-values is impossible); a single-replica RWO Deployment pinned to move nodes deadlocks (old pod holds the volume, new sits ContainerCreating Multi-Attach); deleting the old pod isn't enough (RS recreates it); delete the old ReplicaSet to free the volume; must beat the 20m harbor HR timeout.

We do NOT set updateStrategy: Recreate: the goharbor chart always renders a rollingUpdate block, which Kubernetes rejects alongside type: Recreate'; persistence registry/jobservice accessMode ReadWriteOnce, storageClass longhorn-general (lines 89-96); HR timeout: 20m (line 17).
items.webEvidence: n/a (K8s semantics: a Deployment will not recreate a superseded/old ReplicaSet's pods; rollingUpdate + Recreate are mutually exclusive in the Deployment spec — matches the comment).
items.reasoning: The manifest comment independently confirms the RollingUpdate-hardcoding and Multi-Attach behavior, RWO mounts, and 20m timeout. The 'delete the old ReplicaSet' step is a runtime imperative not in the manifest, but it follows directly from K8s controller semantics (a Deployment owns its current+history RSes but won't resurrect a non-current revision's pods). Consistent.

items.title: helm-controller cache-sync rollback is driven by a loaded control-plane API
items.claim: RWO-move/postRenderer HR upgrades failed with 'failed to wait for object to sync in-cache after patching: context deadline exceeded' → remediateLastFailure rollback loop; after emptying the soyo control-planes of apps the same harbor move succeeded with no rollback; hypothesis: the cache-sync timeout was caused by the slow/loaded soyo apiserver. Needs verification.
items.repoEvidence: kubernetes/apps/security/dependency-track/app/helmrelease.yaml:28-33 corroborates one half: 'A post-render affinity patch was the cause of the prior pin's rollback loop (helm-controller context deadline exceeded on the slow soyo API) — reverted in 2db5b15. Native values apply cleanly.' The emptied-control-plane-then-success narrative for harbor is an operational observation not captured in a manifest.
items.webEvidence: Flux helm-controller is known to emit 'failed to wait for object to sync in-cache ... context deadline exceeded' under apiserver/cache pressure (general Flux behavior), which is consistent with the hypothesis but does not prove the soyo-load causation for this specific incident.
items.reasoning: The error string and the rollback-loop-from-a-postRenderer-patch are corroborated by the repo comment, so the symptom is real. But the digest's own claim is explicitly a hypothesis ('Needs verification', confidence LOW, causation not proven) — the 'emptied control-plane → success' is correlational and confounded with also dropping the postRenderer. Verdict tracks the digest's own honesty: the symptom is CONFIRMED but the causal explanation is unproven.
items.correction: The 'context deadline exceeded' rollback loop and that dropping the postRenderer / unloading the control-plane let the move succeed are real observations; the specific causal claim 'a loaded soyo apiserver caused the cache-sync timeout' remains an unverified hypothesis (the postRenderer was also removed, a confound).
items.docImpact: If written to docs/skills, keep it framed as a hypothesis to investigate, not a rule. Pair it with the confirmed, actionable lesson: a post-render affinity patch (not native nodeSelector) drove DT's rollback loop and was reverted in 2db5b15.

items.title: All Longhorn StorageClasses are now Immediate; longhorn-gitops SC was deleted
items.claim: Every Longhorn SC is volumeBindingMode: Immediate now; WFFC was eliminated because with dataLocality: disabled volumes are network-attached so WFFC's PV-node-locking is pure downside (it excluded worker-1); longhorn-gitops SC retired 2026-06-21; soyos stay Longhorn-free; the storageclass Flux ks uses force: true so the immutable binding-mode change recreates the SC.

items.title: The default longhorn SC still provisions 3 replicas on a 2-storage-node cluster (deliberate deferral)
items.claim: The chart-created default longhorn SC carries numberOfReplicas: 3 (immutable SC param from persistence.defaultClassReplicaCount) but only 2 Longhorn-schedulable nodes with hard anti-affinity exist, so a 3-replica volume can never be healthy; defaultSettings.defaultReplicaCount: 2 does NOT override the explicit SC param; left at 3 deliberately to avoid breaking the HR upgrade; convergence to 2 deferred (ADR-0029 Stage 2 / ADR-0027).

The digest's 'longhorn-hot' phrasing is stale relative to the kustomization — prefer 'longhorn-general' as the canonical 2-replica hot tier in any doc.)

items.title: Post-reboot Longhorn churn self-heals serially; detect rebuilds via JSON rebuildStatus, not table grep
items.claim: Rolling-rebooting all nodes left ~25 volumes degraded that self-healed to ~0 over ~1-2h; each volume wants 2 replicas (one per worker), rebuilds throttled by concurrent-replica-rebuild-per-node-limit=1 (serial) + replica-auto-balance=disabled; grep on the replicas table always returns 0 for rebuild because it's .status.rebuildStatus (JSON only); node-down-pod-deletion-policy=delete-both-statefulset-and-deployment-pod.
items.repoEvidence: kubernetes/apps/longhorn-system/longhorn/app/helmrelease.yaml: concurrentReplicaRebuildPerNodeLimit: "1", replicaAutoBalance: disabled (comment: best-effort auto-balance generated a rebuild storm that OOM-killed longhorn-manager), nodeDownPodDeletionPolicy: delete-both-statefulset-and-deployment-pod; ADR-0026 confirms 2 storage nodes → 2-replica target.
items.webEvidence: Longhorn docs/support confirm rebuildStatus is exposed via the backend API / volume status (not a kubectl table column) and that concurrent-replica-rebuild-per-node-limit controls how many replicas rebuild simultaneously per node (default 5; set to 1 = fully serial). Replica rebuilding self-heals automatically when a replica fails/returns.
items.reasoning: Every setting named (limit=1, auto-balance disabled, the pod-deletion policy) is present in the HR with matching rationale, and upstream confirms rebuildStatus is JSON-only and that limit=1 serializes rebuilds. The ~25-volumes/~1-2h figures are operational observations not independently checkable but plausible and internally consistent with serial rebuild + 2-replica volumes.
items.docImpact: None; the 'compute the 0-healthy-replica check from replica healthyAt/failedAt, not a grep' guidance is a sound, durable gotcha.

summary: Verified all 12 items in B8-flux-observability.md against repo files, live Grafana/k8s MCP, and primary upstream docs. Results: 8 CONFIRMED, 3 PARTIAL, 1 REFUTED. The Grafana alerting core claims (threshold needs top-level expression: query, the 16-rule/3-week outage, count() vs sum() over boolean gauges, `or vector(0)` for empty vectors, ServiceMonitor release+selector labels, the validator scripts wired into CI, the worker-pool component not patching bare Jobs, ADR-0028/0030) are strongly confirmed by exact file lines and live state (error-state rule list is empty; sum(MemoryPressure) has no `or vector(0)` while count() rules do). The one REFUTED item is the Forgejo-runner-logs-not-in-Loki claim: live Loki now has namespace/pod/container labels AND forgejo-runner pods' logs ({pod=~"forgejo-runner.*"} = 2 streams/701 entries) — the alloy pipeline was relabeled since the digest was written, so that gotcha is stale. PARTIAL items: the Sloth burn-rate "linger" is real but oversimplified (MWMB design's short-window AND requirement gives quick reset); the supply-chain count snapshot (159) is now 163 (grows over time, magnitude correct); the sbom-uploader schedule is Sundays 02:10 not 02:00. spegel-only-drift cause is plausible and the unapplied-fix half is confirmed (no ignore block in its HR), but "only HR that drifts" couldn't be live-cross-checked across all HRs.

items.title: spegel is the only HR that perpetually shows DriftDetected — cause + unapplied fix
items.claim: A global Flux patch sets driftDetection.mode: warn on every HR; spegel is the only HR that perpetually drifts (DaemonSet server-defaulted fields revisionHistoryLimit/updateStrategy.rollingUpdate.maxSurge), benign, fix (per-HR driftDetection.ignore) not yet applied.

Promote to enabled per-HR once benign-drift paths are tuned out with spec.driftDetection.ignore'); kubernetes/apps/kube-system/spegel/app/helmrelease.yaml has NO driftDetection block (fix unapplied — confirmed). What I could NOT independently verify is the strong word 'ONLY' — that spegel is the single HR perpetually drifting requires querying live gotk_reconcile_condition across all HRs, which the digest itself marks [VERIFIED] but I could not re-confirm in read scope. items.correction: Confirmed: global mode:warn patch + spegel HR has no ignore block (fix unapplied) + DaemonSet server-default drift is the plausible mechanism. Not re-verified: that spegel is literally the ONLY drifting HR, and the exact third defaulted field. Treat 'only' as the original author's live observation, not a re-checked invariant.

items.title: Grafana threshold alert rules need a top-level expression: <input-refId> or they error every eval (broke all 16 SLO rules ~3 weeks)
items.claim: A GrafanaAlertRuleGroup threshold SSE node errors ('no variable specified to reference for refId threshold') unless its model carries expression: query (bare refId, not $query/A); kubeconform+flux-local don't catch it; guarded by scripts/validate_grafana_alert_expr.py + ADR-0030.
items.repoEvidence: kubernetes/apps/observability/grafana/app/alerting/slo-platform.yaml:34-46 threshold node: `refId: threshold / type: threshold / expression: query / datasource: {uid: "-100", type: __expr__}` — exactly the digest snippet. ADR doc docs/techdocs/docs/adr/adr-0030-grafana-threshold-rule-shape.md states 'All 16 GrafanaAlertRuleGroup SLO rules errored on every evaluation for ~3 weeks', quotes the exact sse.parseError, confirms bare refId `query` (not $query, not A), confirms kubeconform/flux-local can't see the preserve-unknown-fields model, confirms operator chart 5.23.0 + throwaway-MCP validation. scripts/validate_grafana_alert_expr.py enforces it; wired into run-flux-local-test.sh:18 and .github/workflows/e2e.yaml:49.

Live: alerting_manage_rules list states:[error] returned null (no rules erroring now) — the fix works.

Live error-state list is empty, confirming current health. Nothing falsifies the claim.

items.title: Pre-flight a Grafana alert-rule shape with a throwaway MCP rule before mass-editing

The validator+ADR repeatedly state render-time validation cannot see model internals.

items.docImpact: None — accurate and directly actionable; good skill content.

items.title: PromQL anti-patterns: count() over a boolean gauge counts series; empty filtered set returns NoData not 0
items.claim: count(kube_node_status_condition{...status=true}) returns node count regardless of pressure → use sum(); count(up==0)/count(flux_resource_info{ready=False}) return empty vector when healthy → health:nodata → append `or vector(0)`, EXCEPT rules where noDataState:Alerting is intentional.
items.repoEvidence: slo-platform.yaml:242 `sum(kube_node_status_condition{condition="MemoryPressure",status="true"})` (no `or vector(0)` — correct, sum of 0/1s); slo-platform.yaml:294 same for DiskPressure. Empty-vector rules use `or vector(0)`: slo-observability.yaml:25 `count(up == 0) or vector(0)`; slo-platform.yaml:28 `count(flux_resource_info{ready="False",suspended="False"}) or vector(0)`; slo-platform.yaml:80,135,187 likewise; slo-security.yaml:29,81,133,240,292 use sum(...) or vector(0). Exactly matches both snippets and the sum-vs-or-vector(0) distinction.
items.webEvidence: PromQL semantics (Prometheus docs): count() counts the number of series in the vector (so over a per-node 0/1 boolean gauge it yields node count), sum() adds the sample values (yielding the count of true conditions). A filtered instant vector with no matching series is empty; Grafana maps an empty datasource result to the NoData state. `or vector(0)` substitutes a scalar 0 when the left side is empty — standard idiom. All consistent.
items.reasoning: Tried to break the sum/count distinction: kube_node_status_condition emits one series per (node,condition,status) with value 0/1, so count counts nodes and sum counts trues — claim correct. Tried to break NoData: an empty vector → Grafana NoData (not 0) unless `or vector(0)`. The 'EXCEPT noDataState:Alerting intentional' caveat is sound (a watch-the-watchers rule must page on NoData). Repo applies both rules exactly as described.
items.docImpact: None — accurate. Strong, reusable PromQL gotcha for the grafana-dashboard skill.

items.title: Operator-managed Grafana ServiceMonitor needs release label AND operator's actual selector labels
items.claim: For kube-prometheus-stack to scrape grafana-operator-managed Grafana, the SM must carry metadata.labels.release: kube-prometheus-stack AND spec.selector.matchLabels = {app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: grafana-operator, grafana.internal/instance: grafana} (NOT app.kubernetes.io/name: grafana).
items.repoEvidence: kubernetes/apps/observability/grafana/app/servicemonitor.yaml:16 `release: kube-prometheus-stack`; lines 27-29 selector.matchLabels `app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: grafana-operator` + `grafana.internal/instance: grafana`. The file's own comment (lines 12-15) explains the release label is required by kube-prometheus-stack's SM selection and ties it to the 3-week outage blind spot. The grafana.internal/instance: grafana label is consistently the operator instance selector across folders/datasources/dashboards (grep showed it everywhere; app.kubernetes.io/name: grafana is only a cosmetic metadata label on the SM itself, line 11).
items.webEvidence: kube-prometheus-stack default serviceMonitorSelector matches on the helm release label (release: <release-name>) unless serviceMonitorSelectorNilUsesHelmValues is disabled — well-established prometheus-operator behavior. grafana-operator labels its managed Service with app.kubernetes.io/managed-by=grafana-operator and grafana.internal/instance=<cr-name>; selecting on app.kubernetes.io/name would not match the operator-created Service. Consistent with repo.
items.reasoning: Tried to break 'NOT app.kubernetes.io/name': the SM's own metadata carries app.kubernetes.io/name: grafana (line 11) but the spec.selector deliberately uses managed-by+instance — so the distinction the digest draws (selector must use the operator's real labels, name alone won't match the Service) is exactly what the file does. Note the SM comment cites ADR-0031 for this point (digest didn't pin an ADR number, fine).
items.docImpact: None substantive. Minor: the in-repo provenance for this point is ADR-0031, not 0030 — a docs cross-reference could note both.

items.title: Sloth burn-rate / synthetic alerts linger after recovery; disable alerts/SLOs in lock-step with their workload
items.claim: Multi-window burn-rate SLO alerts (Sloth, fed by probe_success) keep firing after recovery because the burned budget is still in the long window — check probe_success before treating as live; k6 SLO + K6CanaryMetricsMissing were left active and should be commented out alongside the k6 suspension; keep slo-synthetic-availability.
items.repoEvidence: k6 lock-step disable IS applied: kubernetes/apps/observability/sloth/slos/kustomization.yaml:11 `# - ./slo-synthetic-k6-canary.yaml` with note 'Disabled alongside the k6 suspension (observability/kustomization.yaml, 2026-06-19)... Re-enable with k6'; slo-synthetic-availability.yaml stays active (kustomization line 8). K6CanaryMetricsMissing rule lives in prometheusrule-synthetic-k6-canaries.yaml:82, and that whole PrometheusRule is commented out at kube-prometheus-stack/app/kustomization.yaml:14 `# - ./rules/prometheusrule-synthetic-k6-canaries.yaml`. So the prescribed fix is in place. Sloth SLOs use probe_success: sloth/slos/slo-synthetic-availability.yaml:21,67,113.
items.webEvidence: https://sloth.dev/slo-plugins/core/alert_rules_v1/ + Google SRE workbook: Sloth MWMB alerts require BOTH long AND short window over threshold (AND), and the short window gives quick reset after recovery — explicitly designed to NOT linger long. So 'keeps firing because the long window is still elevated' is an oversimplification: the short-window AND requirement should clear it quickly; persistent re-firing is more a flapping/repeated-breach effect within the long window.
items.reasoning: The DECISION half (disable k6 SLO + K6CanaryMetricsMissing in lock-step, keep slo-synthetic-availability) is fully confirmed in-repo. The FACT half ('burn-rate alerts linger after recovery because budget is in the long window') is technically imprecise for standard Sloth MWMB rules, which AND the long and short windows precisely to avoid lingering. The practical advice ('check probe_success current value before treating as a live outage') remains sound regardless.
items.correction: The decision/lock-step part is accurate and already shipped. Refine the mechanism: Sloth MWMB page rules require long AND short window above threshold, so a recovered endpoint clears the short window and the alert resolves fairly quickly; what you actually see is short-window re-breaches or warning/ticket (long-window) rules staying noisy, not the page rule 'lingering' indefinitely. The actionable habit (check probe_success now) is correct.
items.docImpact: Adjust the explanation: replace 'keeps firing because budget is still in the long window' with 'MWMB alerts use long-AND-short windows; the short window resolves pages quickly after recovery, but long-window ticket/warning rules and re-breaches can stay noisy — check probe_success before treating as live'. Keep the lock-step disable guidance verbatim.

items.repoEvidence: AppsTier exclusion regex exact: kubernetes/apps/observability/kube-prometheus-stack/app/prometheusrule-platform-nodes.yaml:85 `pod!~".*-(runner|metrics-exporter|sbom-uploader|policy-bootstrap)-.*"` (and :84 excludes -db-) — bootstrap/provisioner pods not excluded. HelmRelease/Deployment/StatefulSet/CNPG' and does NOT target Job/CronJob. Consistent.

Exclusion regex matches char-for-char. The double-alert claim holds: custom EtcdDbHighFragmentationRatio + the well-known stock rule on the identical metric. ADR-0028 is 'Proposed' not 'Accepted', but the digest only cites the number.

summary: Verified all 18 items in the B9 digest against repo files, Kyverno/Claude-Code/MkDocs/gh-cli primary sources, and reasoning. Result: 12 CONFIRMED, 5 PARTIAL, 1 CORRECTED, 0 REFUTED/UNVERIFIABLE. The Kyverno-mechanics core (deprecated validationFailureAction→failureAction completed in v1.13; autogen <rule>+autogen-<rule> waiver requirement; no per-rule action knob; the 6-policy allowlist hole and its closure via kind-discovery + check-kyverno-test-coverage.sh; ADR-0033) is strongly corroborated by repo source and Kyverno docs. The safety-hook, worktree (.worktreeinclude, --worktree defaults, baseRef, v2.1.49), posture-counts snapshot, lefthook, gh-api-404-to-stdout, and mkdocs-relative-link claims all check out. PARTIALs are precision issues: the guard hook is regex/word-based (not literal "string-match") and does NOT block kubectl get or recoverable pod/job deletes; the coverage gate exempts a baselined policy (storage-cnpg-governance); the worktree PR path is pr-<n> not worktree-<n>; some cross-repo specifics (mkdocs-redirects Dockerfile, github-script ZWSP escape, .releaserc.js) are not in this repo. The one CORRECTED item: the ADR convention claim "decisions get a number only when ratified / pending ones are unnumbered candidates" is contradicted in-tree — ADR-0033 and ADR-0034 are numbered yet status Proposed.

items.title: Safety hook blocks kubectl/Longhorn/talosctl mutations (string-match — even --help/comments)
items.claim: .claude/hooks/guard-destructive.sh blocks kubectl patch/delete/edit/apply/scale/uncordon, Longhorn vol/replica/node mutations, destructive talosctl regardless of in-chat approval; match is string-based so 'talosctl upgrade --help' and an echo comment containing 'upgrade' got blocked; read-only get/describe/logs/exec/--raw and talosctl get/version/etcd are fine.
items.repoEvidence: .claude/hooks/guard-destructive.sh:21 (--dry-run exits 0), :26-31 (kubectl apply/replace/patch/edit/scale/cordon/drain/uncordon/taint/annotate/label/set/create/rollout blocked; delete only of PROTECTED types — namespace/pvc/pv/node/deploy/sts/ds/secret/hr/ks/crd/gateway/httproute/-f), :35 (talosctl reset|apply-config|bootstrap|wipe|upgrade(-k8s)?|edit machineconfig), :33-34 (helm/flux delete). It uses 'grep -qE' (regex) on a space-collapsed command line.
items.webEvidence: none (repo-local hook behavior)
items.reasoning: The block IS regex/word-based and IS in-tree, so the 'even --help and comments get blocked' anecdote is plausible: 'talosctl upgrade --help' matches the talosctl regex (no --help carve-out beyond --dry-run), and a line echoing 'upgrade' near 'talosctl' could match. But two precision errors: (1) it is NOT a pure literal string-match — it's ERE with word/boundary anchors; (2) the hook does NOT block 'kubectl get' nor 'kubectl delete pod/job' (recoverable deletes are intentionally allowed — only the protected-type list and -f are denied), and there is NO Longhorn-specific rule in this file at all. So 'Longhorn volume/replica/node mutations' blocking is not evidenced by THIS file.
items.correction: guard-destructive.sh is REGEX/word-based (grep -qE on the collapsed command), not a literal string-match. It blocks kubectl apply/replace/patch/edit/scale/cordon/drain/uncordon/taint/annotate/label/set/create-(f|namespace|secret)/rollout-(restart|undo) and kubectl delete of a protected resource TYPE (recoverable pod/job deletes are allowed), plus helm/flux delete and destructive talosctl. It does NOT carve out --help, so help/comment text containing a trigger word can trip it. Longhorn-specific blocking is not in this file; Longhorn mutations are caught only insofar as they go through kubectl patch/delete-of-protected-type.
items.docImpact: Refine the skill/memory note: say 'regex/word-based, no --help exemption' (not 'string-match'), and don't claim a dedicated Longhorn rule — Longhorn is covered only via the generic kubectl mutation/protected-delete rules.
